title: US-Saudi Nuclear Deal Reshapes Gulf Power Map, Trump Threatens Iranian Infrastructure Over Hormuz, Houthi Blockade Chokes Red Sea Oil Route
subhead: Washington signs a landmark nuclear cooperation agreement with Riyadh that could allow Saudi uranium enrichment, even as the US-Iran war enters its 12th night and a Houthi maritime embargo forces tankers into sharp U-turns away from Bab al-Mandab.

sections.heading: US-Saudi Nuclear Deal Marks a Middle East Power Shift
sections.body:
The United States and Saudi Arabia signed a landmark nuclear cooperation agreement on Wednesday that could allow the Gulf kingdom to enrich uranium on its own soil — a first for any US partner and a decision that could reshape the regional balance for decades. The deal, announced by the US Department of Energy, is described as a 'peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement' that will give American firms 'great access' to Saudi Arabia's nuclear energy programme. A bilateral safeguards agreement was also signed alongside it.

According to US media reports, the deal does not require Saudi Arabia to recognise Israel — dropping a condition that had stalled negotiations under previous administrations. It may also permit Saudi Arabia to enrich uranium domestically rather than importing fuel, a provision that distinguishes it from the 2009 US-UAE agreement under which Abu Dhabi accepted strict non-proliferation limits. Energy Secretary Chris Wright said the agreements 'uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation,' but experts and former Israeli officials voiced concern that domestic enrichment could eventually give Riyadh a path to a nuclear weapon, even if that remains years or decades away.

Rosemary Kelanic of the Defense Priorities think tank said it would be 'quite shocking' if the US allowed Saudi enrichment, noting: 'The US has never done that before — if you can produce your own nuclear fuel, you become a much bigger risk' for building weapons. Democratic Senator Edward Markey condemned the deal, saying Trump and Rubio's 'hypocrisy is matched only by their recklessness' in allowing Saudi nuclear technology while waging war on Iran over its nuclear programme. The deal now heads to Congress for review, where it faces opposition from both sides of the aisle. The timing — mid-war with Iran, amid a Houthi blockade of Saudi ports — gives Riyadh extraordinary leverage and raises the strategic stakes of US-Saudi ties at a moment when both countries are under attack from Iran.
sections.whyItMatters: A US-backed Saudi enrichment programme would break decades of non-proliferation precedent and could trigger a regional nuclear chain reaction. The deal also signals that Washington is willing to trade nuclear access for alliance loyalty during wartime — a calculus Tehran will not miss.

sections.heading: Trump Threatens Iran's Infrastructure as US Strikes Enter 12th Night
sections.body:
President Donald Trump warned on Wednesday that the US would destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant — potentially near Tehran — every time Iranian forces attack a ship in the Strait of Hormuz, 'whether it be by missile, rocket, drone, or any other device or weapon.' The threat, posted on Truth Social, represents an explicit escalation: the US is now tying concrete civilian infrastructure targets to specific Iranian actions at sea.

The statement came as the US military carried out its 12th consecutive night of strikes on Iran. Explosions were reported near Tehran, in the north-western city of Tabriz, the south-eastern ports of Chabahar and Konarak, and in Bushehr, home to Iran's only operational nuclear power plant. Iran's health ministry says US attacks since late June have killed 53 people, including three children, and wounded 590. Trump also said the next US target could be the underground complex known as Pickaxe Mountain, near Iran's Natanz enrichment site, where Iran is suspected of building an undeclared enrichment facility.

Iran responded with its own escalation. Chief negotiator Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf declared: 'In a region where we do not sell oil, no one will sell oil. If our security is not ensured, no infrastructure will be safe.' Kuwaiti air defences engaged Iranian drones on Wednesday, while Bahrain and Jordan also reported intercepting Iranian missile and drone strikes. A fourth US service member killed in the Middle East was identified — Sgt Angel S Rampersad, 28, previously declared missing after an Iranian strike in Jordan — bringing total US fatalities to 18. Trump attended a dignified transfer ceremony at Dover Air Force Base. Secretary of State Rubio, speaking in Manila at the ASEAN forum, warned that Iran's demand to control the Strait of Hormuz 'would set a dangerous precedent' for freedom of navigation worldwide.
sections.whyItMatters: Both sides are now explicitly threatening civilian infrastructure — a line that, once crossed, makes de-escalation harder and broadens the war beyond military targets. The tit-for-tat framing (one Iranian action = one US strike on Iranian infrastructure) raises the risk of rapid escalation spirals.

sections.heading: Houthi Blockade Forces Tanker U-Turns as Red Sea Route Narrows
sections.body:
At least seven oil tankers made sharp U-turns near the Bab al-Mandab Strait after the Houthis declared a 'maritime embargo' against Saudi Arabia on Monday, according to BBC Verify analysis of ship-tracking data. The vessels, carrying Saudi crude bound for China and India, reversed course rather than transit the strait — effectively closing the backup route Saudi Arabia had relied on since the Strait of Hormuz became impassable.

Saudi Arabia had diverted more than 70% of its crude exports through its east-west pipeline to the Red Sea port of Yanbu since the Iran war shut down Hormuz. About four million barrels per day have been shipped from Yanbu in recent weeks, compared with 973,000 a year earlier, according to Kpler and Signal Ocean data. The Houthi announcement — retaliation for a Saudi blockade of Houthi-controlled Yemeni ports — now threatens this lifeline too. The EU's naval force Aspides has recommended that 'merchant vessels linked to Israeli, US or Saudi interests avoid transiting the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden until the threat level decreases.'

Kpler analyst Naveen Das told BBC Verify that the only remaining option for Saudi crude heading to Asia would be the Mediterranean route around Africa's southern tip — adding weeks and significant cost. 'We could weather it for two or three weeks, even a month, but even in that time, we will see higher freight rates, higher energy prices, and that bleeds into higher consumer costs,' Das said. Oil prices reached $94 per barrel on Wednesday in response to the escalation. Trump separately threatened to 'take care of' the Houthis if they followed through on the blockade.
sections.whyItMatters: With both Hormuz and Bab al-Mandab under threat, Saudi Arabia — the world's top oil exporter — has no safe maritime route to Asian markets. The economic ripple effects will hit consumers within weeks, not months.

sections.heading: Ukraine's New Military Chief Vows Expanded Offensive Operations
sections.body:
Mykhailo Drapatyi, appointed commander of Ukraine's armed forces on Wednesday after President Zelensky ousted the previous chief amid protests, said the military would 'continue and intensify offensive operations in all domains' — including missions behind enemy lines. The pledge came as Ukrainian drone strikes hit Wildberries warehouses in Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk in southern Russia, the second wave of attacks on the retail giant's logistics hubs this week.

The first strikes over the weekend hit Wildberries facilities in Elektrostal near Moscow and Kotovsk in the Tambov region, killing eight workers and destroying goods worth an estimated £750m. Ukraine says the warehouses stored 'sanctioned components for drone production and navigation equipment,' though independent confirmation is lacking. The attacks have exposed the economic toll on Russia's small businesses: 209,000 small and medium-sized companies closed in the first quarter of 2026, according to Kontur.Fokus, while oil and gas revenues are 23% lower than the first half of 2025.

Zelensky also reported Ukrainian strikes on an oil depot, a tanker, and four cargo ships from Russia's 'shadow fleet' in the Black Sea and Sea of Azov. Russia's defence ministry claimed its own strikes on Ukrainian port infrastructure at Odesa and Chornomorsk, and said its forces captured the settlements of Artilne in Kharkiv and Blahodatne in Zaporizhzhia. Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov dismissed Drapatyi's appointment as unlikely to change the frontline situation.

On the diplomatic front, Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov confirmed he would meet US counterpart Marco Rubio on Thursday in Manila on the sidelines of the ASEAN gathering. Rubio said he would 'see if there was an opportunity to renew Ukraine peace talks,' but expectations are low — Washington's focus remains squarely on the Iran war.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine is proving it can bring the economic cost of war home to Russian civilians, but the frontline remains largely static. The Lavrov-Rubio meeting is the first diplomatic contact on Ukraine in months, though neither side signals meaningful flexibility.

sections.heading: US Accuses China's Moonshot AI of Stealing American Model Capabilities
sections.body:
White House science and technology adviser Michael Kratsios accused Beijing-based Moonshot AI of a 'large scale' effort to steal capabilities from top US AI models through distillation — the process of extracting outputs from a stronger model to train a weaker one. Kratsios said Moonshot AI 'distilled' capabilities from Anthropic's models for the development of Kimi K3, a Chinese AI model that gained global attention after its release last week, with many analysts believing it has narrowed the gap between Western and Chinese AI.

The accusation came a day after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the US would examine whether Chinese AI models have stolen capabilities from American rivals, warning: 'If we see that overseas models are stealing from our great companies, we have the ability to sanction them.' The increased scrutiny coincides with preparations for a Trump-Xi meeting expected in September.

In a separate development, Anthropic agreed to pay authors $1.5 billion to settle a lawsuit over pirated books used to train AI chatbots, according to AP — one of the largest copyright settlements in the AI industry to date. The dual storylines — US accusing China of AI theft while a major US AI company settles its own copyright violations — underscore the tangled ethics of AI training data in an intensifying geopolitical contest.
sections.whyItMatters: The AI rivalry between the US and China is moving from export controls to accusations of intellectual property theft. If Washington sanctions Moonshot AI, it would mark a new front in the technology cold war — and could accelerate the fragmentation of global AI development into competing ecosystems.

sections.heading: House Passes $95 Billion Iran War Budget as Oil Climbs and Diplomacy Stalls
sections.body:
House Republicans approved a $95 billion budget package on Wednesday that would largely fund the Iran war, along with a Trump-backed bill changing US voting regulations. The measure passed with only Republican support and now faces an uphill battle in the narrowly divided Senate. The vote came as Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate Appropriations Committee that military training would have to be curtailed unless lawmakers approved an urgent funding boost — the war has already cost $37.5 billion, according to the Pentagon.

Iran's chief negotiator Ghalibaf warned that the 'equation of this war is clear: either all or none,' while Secretary of State Rubio — speaking at the ASEAN forum in Manila — said the US was 'open to diplomacy' but that Iran did not appear serious about talks. Rubio also warned Asian leaders that Iran's demands over the Strait of Hormuz would set a 'dangerous precedent' for freedom of navigation, linking the Gulf conflict directly to Asian economic interests.

The diplomatic picture remains grim. Pakistan-mediated efforts to salvage the June ceasefire MoU have shown no progress. Iran insists it will not reopen Hormuz without security guarantees, while the US demands unconditional clearance of the strait. Neither side appears willing to offer the concessions the other requires, and the fighting — now in its fifth month — has settled into a grinding rhythm of nightly US strikes and Iranian regional retaliation.
sections.whyItMatters: The $95 billion package signals open-ended US commitment to the Iran campaign, but Senate passage is uncertain. Meanwhile, the diplomatic track is effectively frozen — both sides are escalating, and no mediator has sufficient credibility or leverage to bridge the gap.

bottomLine: The US-Saudi nuclear deal is the day's most consequential development: it breaks decades of non-proliferation precedent, drops the Israel-recognition condition, and could allow Riyadh to enrich uranium — all while the US wages war on Iran over precisely that issue. The hypocrisy is not lost on Tehran, or on US lawmakers who will now have to vote on both a nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia and a $95 billion war budget against Iran's nuclear ambitions. Meanwhile, the dual closure of Hormuz and Bab al-Mandab leaves the world's top oil exporter with no safe maritime route, Trump has explicitly tied Iranian civilian infrastructure to shipping attacks, and Ukraine's new commander is vowing deeper strikes into Russia. The war is expanding on every axis — and the off-ramps are narrowing.
